title: ultraDocumentBody
body:
Welcome to Administrative Law!

We are looking forward to working with you this semester as we explore this important area of law. 

To get started in this course, please read the Course Profile. The Course Profile will give you an overview of the aims, learning activities and assessment for this course. Make sure you check your timetable to know when and where to attend your classes. 

Administrative law is concerned with the legal accountability of bodies wielding public power/discharging public functions and ensuring that people's rights and interests are respected in their dealings with governments and their delegates or agents. Public power encompasses administrative, judicial and legislative power, and this course is primarily concerned with accountability for the exercise of administrative (executive) power in a range of settings: for example, in respect of matters of life and death (refugee protection), national security, people's health and well-being (social security and child protection), professional and business regulation, environmental protection, education, and taxation. 

Lectures will begin in week 1, and workshops commence in week 2 of the semester.

For the first 4 weeks of the semester, lectures will be for 2 hours per week. There will be no lectures in weeks 5-7. Weekly lectures will resume in week 8 of semester (ie the week beginning 13 April) for 1 hour per week. Lectures will be recorded to assist your revision. In-person attendance at lectures is strongly encouraged. 

Workshops will be weekly although there will be no workshops in weeks 7 and 8 (ie in the weeks beginning 13 and 20 April). Workshops commence again in week 9 (ie the week beginning 27 April). 

The first form of assessment will be an essay that is worth 40% of the overall marks. It will require students to demonstrate their understanding of material covered in workshops 1-5 of the course and the related lectures. The essay topic will be released on Thursday 26 March 2026 at 2pm, and will be due on Thursday 23 April 2026 at 2pm.

In relation to this assessment, the use of generative (predictive) software is not prohibited but is subject to certain strict rules.  The use of predictive software is a fast-moving area in legal education and for the legal profession.  The Law School has recently issued an excellent policy document outlining the competing ethical and practical issues raised by predictive, large language model, software.    Please read this document carefully.  You also now have access, via the UQ Library website, to the Lexis AI tool known as "Protégé".  There is training available in relation to this software which we strongly encourage you to do.

Law School - Use of AI by Students in Law (20Feb26).pdf

The second form of assessment will be an end-of-semester examination (worth 60%) which will focus, largely, on lectures in the second half of the course and the final five workshop topics (workshops 6-10): Judicial (Legality) Review Grounds (3) and Human Rights; Access to Justice (1-2); Remedies; and Automated Decision-Making. There will be some overlap with material covered in earlier topics but the main focus will be on the final five workshop topics and related lectures.

As mentioned above, workshops will begin in week 2. Students are to attend only the workshop in which they are enrolled.

This is a 2-unit course. Under University policy, a total workload of approximately 10 hours per week (including class contact time) is expected for satisfactory progress.

Wishing you all the very best in Administrative Law in 2026,

 Anthony, Peter and Matt
